career_angle: Seeing astrology in your tech newsletter is a red flag that editorial standards have slipped. Astrology predictions have zero predictive power—useful mainly as a case study in how good UX can dress up pseudoscience.

factors.name: Source Authority
factors.rationale: Ben's Bites is credible for AI/tech coverage, but astrology is neither. Covering a birth chart tool in a tech newsletter suggests either poor editorial judgment or aggressive listicle-ification.

factors.name: Evidence Quality
factors.rationale: Zero citations to peer review, studies, or data. References only "classical astrology texts"—essentially saying 'trust old beliefs.' No benchmarks, no validation, no mechanism.

factors.name: Reproducibility
factors.rationale: Birth chart readings are subjective opinion. No two astrologers agree on personality traits from the same chart. Completely unfalsifiable claims.

factors.name: Conflict of Interest
factors.rationale: This is a thinly-veiled product FAQ for AstroSeek.org. Entire piece walks through their features and benefits. Classic advertorial disguised as journalism.

factors.name: Consensus
factors.rationale: Scientific consensus: astrology does not predict behavior or future events. Hundreds of controlled studies show astrology performs at chance level. This contradicts expert consensus entirely.

factors.name: Specificity
factors.rationale: Uses specific terms (planets, houses, Sun/Moon) which sounds technical, but claims remain vague and unfalsifiable. 'Personality insights based on your chart' = horoscope reading.

factors.name: Track Record
factors.rationale: Astrology's track record is abysmal—centuries of failed predictions. Ben's Bites is usually reliable for tech, but this story isn't tech; it's snake oil.
